aurelia_convo_1_n01.npcDialogue: Shut the door. Drusus is dead — stabbed in his own vestibule last night. They're calling it a robbery. A robbery. As if some common cutpurse just happened to open the femoral artery of the one tribune standing between Rome and catastrophe. Someone with good anatomy and better connections wanted that man quiet. Now he's quiet. The allies' last advocate in this city is bleeding into a mosaic floor. Tell me you see what comes next.

responses.playerDialogue: War. They asked for justice with words and we answered with a blade. They've no reason left to ask politely.
The time for speeches is over. Whatever comes next gets settled in iron.
Whoever ordered that knife just kicked over the board. The question is whether they have a plan for what comes next — or just lit a fire they can't stamp out.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: Nothing good. Tell me the rest of it.

aurelia_convo_1_n02.npcDialogue: The Marsi have raised their eagle. Poppaedius Silo — your father drank with that man, do you remember? He sat in that chair — is marching on Rome with thirty thousand men behind him. The Samnites have joined. Asculum butchered every Roman citizen inside its walls, down to the children. This is not a border incident, Gaius. This is the Social War, and our estates sit square in the throat of it. The accounts from Arpinum came this morning. The tenants have stopped paying. Some have fled. Everything your father spent thirty patient years assembling could be ash by autumn.

responses.playerDialogue: Then we save what we can save. The tenants, the household — they're ours before any ambition is.
War means commands. Commands mean advancement. More men have risen in one campaign season than in a decade of peacetime hand-shaking.
Estates change hands in wartime. So do alliances. If we're sharp about this, we come out the other side owning more than we went in with.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: Tell me what you need, Mother.

aurelia_convo_1_n03.npcDialogue: I need you to decide. Now — tonight — because this city makes its choices fast and punishes the men who hesitate. Every able body is being called up. The consuls are raising legions, Sulla's already south of the Liris. But the household needs guarding too — I can hold things here, but someone has to reach Arpinum before the Samnites do. And there are men already circling the wreckage, buying distressed land for a fraction, cornering the grain supply. Tell me plainly. Where will you be?

options.playerDialogue: With the legions. If Rome is at war, I belong in the field. The family name is worth nothing if there's no Republic left to speak it in.
On the road to Arpinum. I'll secure the estate, get the tenants behind walls, then come back. Our people first.
Right here. The real war runs through Rome — supply contracts, intelligence, positioning. Let other men swing gladii while I make sure we survive.

aurelia_convo_1_n04.npcDialogue: I knew you'd say that. I've been dreading it since you were old enough to hold a sword. Go, then. Serve well. Win something, if you can. But write to me — properly, not those three-line dispatches your uncle used to send. And don't throw yourself at some hill fort for the sake of a triumph you're ten years too young to receive. Your father died in his own bed. That is rarer than you think for a Roman man.

options.playerDialogue: I'll come home, Mother. That's a promise.

aurelia_convo_1_n05.npcDialogue: That is your father's answer. I loved him for it then and I love you for it now. Three generations of families work that land — they're our people, not just our tenants. If you can get the harvest stores behind walls, get the women and children to high ground — you'll have done more genuine good than every senator currently screaming about Italian treachery. Go quickly. The Via Latina won't be safe another week.

options.playerDialogue: I leave at first light. Hold things here — I'll send word from the road.

aurelia_convo_1_n06.npcDialogue: Cold. But correct. Generals win battles — the men who fund and supply them win wars. I'll keep the household running. You keep us positioned. But understand this: there will be men who call you a coward for staying behind while others bleed. Make very sure your results are loud enough to shut their mouths.

options.playerDialogue: Let them talk. I'll be the one still standing when the laurels are handed out.

aurelia_convo_1_n07.npcDialogue: See that you do. Take your father's cloak — the one from Narbo. It doesn't look like much, but it's warmer than anything the army will give you.

aurelia_convo_1_n08.npcDialogue: I've held this house through worse than war. Almost worse. Be careful on the road, Gaius. And if you get there and the Samnites are already inside the walls — turn around. The land is not worth you. Nothing is worth you.

aurelia_convo_1_n09.npcDialogue: See that you are. This family has survived three centuries by being useful to the right people at the right moment. Be useful now. But be careful — the men who profit most from a war are always the first names on the list when it's over.

deltas.reason: You chose the battlefield over safety

operations.reason: Your willingness to fight reassures the traditional establishment

deltas.reason: You put duty to family above personal ambition
You chose to exploit the chaos rather than fight in it
